title: How Kubernetes works internally
author: Priya
content:
Take Quiz

Kubernetes

Kubernetes is a container orchestration platform used to deploy, manage, and scale containerized applications. It performs health monitoring of containers running inside pods and ensures the desired state of the application is maintained. If a pod fails, becomes unhealthy, or a node goes down, Kubernetes can automatically recreate or reschedule replacement pods based on the configured replica count.

  • It's a container orchestration tool and does proper health check-up of all the container pods and can be integrated with tools like- Prometheus, Grafana to prepare a health metric report as per the resource utilization, network traffic, latency, and other observability data.
  • It decouple all such pods place a replica of that when any of the pod encounter an error or any performance issue.
  • It continuously monitors cluster resources, pod status, and node health through its control plane components of worker and master nodes. Kubernetes supports automatic scaling and downscaling of pods using mechanisms such as Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)/ cluster Autoscaler.
  • Kubernetes does pod scheduling (assigning pods to nodes) through scheduler .

How Kubernetes internally do Pods resizing

How to decide number of instances of pods/nodes to add/remove in Kubernetes as per resource utilisation?
Kubernetes monitors resources like - CPU,memory and other metrics to decide if resource usage has exceed its permissible limit than it adds more number of similar instances of replica pods/nodes.
It does 2 types of scaling -
1.Horizotal Pod Scling
2.Cluster Scaling

  • HPA - A permissible limit is set with percentage of utilisation of its components.eg... CPU utilisation limit is set 70% than , Scaling will be done as -
    pods required = (Curent pod Count) * (Current CPU usage)/(Expected CPU usage)
    considering , current pod count = 4 , and CPU utilisation has reached to 140%.
    than ,
    pods required = 4*140/70 = 8
    and , K8s automatically reduced pod count when CPU utilisation go below an expected limit.
  • CA - If there r still some capacity issue of CPU or memory and cluster capacity requirement is not met after adding maximum replica of pods than another node will be added to meet the requirement . We call them worker node.
    Min. replicas of pod set as - 3
    Max. replicas of pod set as - 10
  • This is how pods scaling is done with controller manager who creates new podes and scheduler assign those pods to nodes.

Components of Master and worker nodes of Kubernetes

All pods deployment and management among nodes is done by master node by checking the health and capability of which worker node has the capacity to produce new pod/node and also check which worker node needs replacement pod , adding up or downsizing of pods.

Master Node = etcd + API Server + Controller Manager + Scheduler

  • etcd db - It's a configuration db that stores configuration data and state of worker nodes.
  • API server - Performs operation on cluster nodes.It receives request and do API call by making communication with other components to serve the request.
  • Controller Manager - It manages all the clusters and watch for changes and action to take place to bring the cluster to the desired state.
  • Scheduler - Responsible for distribution of workload among pods/nodes.
Worker Node = Docker Runtime Env + Kube proxy + Kubelet

  • Docker Runtime Env - It provides a runtime environment for the containers and pulling image from docker hun and containerise our application by deploying it onto container.
  • Kubelet - Runs on every worker node to deploy and manage pods in K8s cluster.It communicate with controller manager , read configuration from etcd db.and decide how to manage these pods
  • Kube Proxy - For pod to pod communication, load balancing by routing traffic to the right container.Act as a service proxy by minimizing latency with faster response to user queries.

Scheduler - If request comes from user end to add more pods than scheduler checks every worker node to see which node has enough capacity to launch a new pod. and will provide info. of that worker node to API server which inturn gives this info to kubelet of that worker node and thus kubelet will create new node as instructed by API server.
API server - is the central point for all other components.Any request comes from the user's end or from any of the component of master/worker node is handled by API server.It is like front end controller for all the services. An entrypoint to every request that is done inside K8s cluster.If DevOps engineer wants to do any manual configuration that could be adding/removing of pods , it has to talk to API server which will validate our request ,authenticate us and will give us the response.
Controller Manager - It manages the entire cluster.If any of the pod is not in its desired state like - any of the resources of that pod r not properly utlised than request of that worker node is sent to API server and it will ask the Kubelet to made that change in its worker node by adding/removing pods.
A controller manager has factory of controllers under it. HPA controller ,Node Controller ,replicaset controller , job controller , deployment controller ...

  1. if reuirement comes for 3 replica pods - replicaset controller will meet up with that requirement.
  2. If any worker node died - Node controller will send its info to controller manager , Controller Manager will create replacement.
  3. if resource utilisation.eg...CPU is high.low - HPA controller will add/remove pods to maintain the desired state.

Simple Analogy

ComponentsAnalogy
Controller MangerCEO
SchedulerHead HR
KubeletLocal Branch Manager

- Scheduler decides WHERE pods should run.
- Controller Manager decides WHAT state should exist.
- Kubelet actually MAKES it happen on a node.
